verses.text: Does Job know when the wild goats give birth or watch over the calving deer in the hills? God tends creatures no shepherd sees. His care reaches the secret places of the wilderness, far from human eyes.

Does Job know when the wild goats give birth or watch over the calving deer in the hills? God tends creatures no shepherd sees. His care reaches the secret places of the wilderness, far from human eyes.

Who set the wild donkey free to roam the wastes, scorning the city's noise? God gave it its untamed life. Even the creatures man cannot harness live wholly under their Maker's hand.

Will the wild ox consent to serve Job, plough his fields, or bring home his harvest? Its strength is not at man's command. God alone governs the powers we cannot tame.

The ostrich flaps proudly yet leaves her eggs in the dust, forgetting that a foot may crush them. Her folly is part of her making. God's wisdom is shown even in a creature's strange want of it.

She is hard toward her young as if they were not hers, because God withheld wisdom from her — yet when she runs, she outpaces the horse. He gives and withholds as He pleases. His sovereignty assigns each creature its gifts and lacks.

Did Job give the horse his might and the snorting terror of his neck? The warhorse laughs at fear and charges undismayed. Its courage too is the gift of God, not of man.

Amid rattling quivers and flashing spears he says, as it were, 'Ha, ha,' smelling the battle from afar. His fierce spirit is God-given. The very boldness men admire was placed in him by his Maker.

Does the hawk fly south by Job's wisdom, or the eagle mount and build her nest on high at his word? Their instincts come from God. The heights and the hunting of the birds declare a wisdom not our own.
